worth_it_explanation: Creative blocking is where a publisher decides which advertisers, product categories, and creative types may run on its inventory through Index Exchange. A block list that drifts away from the publisher's actual standards lets through ads the publisher meant to keep out, or suppress revenue by blocking far more than the standards require. The check is a review of the blocking controls a publisher already maintains in the Index Exchange console, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific block or gap the publisher can act on.
intro: Creative blocking on Index Exchange (IX) is the set of controls a publisher uses to keep unwanted ads off its inventory, covering advertiser brands, advertiser domains, product categories, and creative types. The platform applies a baseline of default blocks for prohibited content, and the publisher layers its own blocks on top to match its brand-safety standards and any direct-sold commitments. This subcategory looks at whether those blocks are maintained against a current, written standard and whether they are scoped tightly enough to protect the inventory without suppressing more demand than the standard calls for.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are not maintained against a current brand-safety standard
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Index Exchange applies default blocks for prohibited content, but the advertiser, category, and domain blocks that match a publisher's own standards are the publisher's to maintain. When those blocks are not kept against a written, current standard, ads the publisher meant to exclude can run on its inventory, which can break a direct-sold exclusivity commitment or place an off-brand ad next to its content.

educate.base:
Index Exchange (IX) splits ad blocking into two layers. The platform maintains a baseline of default blocks that screens out prohibited content such as fake system warnings, illegal products, hate speech, and malicious landing pages, and that baseline applies to every publisher. On top of it, the publisher maintains its own blocks for the advertisers, product categories, and creative types it does not want on its inventory. The default layer protects against the obviously harmful. The publisher layer is what carries the publisher's own brand-safety standard, and it is the layer this check is about.

The publisher layer only does its job when it tracks a current, written standard. Brand-safety standards move. A publisher signs a category sponsorship that requires a competitor block, a direct-sold advertiser negotiates exclusivity that has to be enforced against the open auction, a regulator or an advertiser relationship makes a product category off-limits, or an internal policy changes which industries the publisher will accept. When the standard moves and the blocks do not, the gap shows up as an ad the publisher meant to exclude running on its inventory. Because the open auction fills impressions automatically, that gap is invisible until someone reports the ad or reads the served-creative data.

Index Exchange supports blocks along several labels, so the standard can be enforced at the level it was written. A publisher can block an advertiser brand, an advertiser domain (the adomain), a product or industry category, a creative type, or a specific creative ID. The audit question is whether each block in place maps to a current line in the publisher's written standard, and whether each line in the standard is reflected by a block.

A few signs point to block lists that have drifted from the standard:
  • No current written brand-safety standard exists to compare the live blocks against
  • Blocks reference advertisers, categories, or commitments that have lapsed, while newer commitments have no matching block
  • A direct-sold exclusivity or category sponsorship is on record but no corresponding block enforces it against the open auction
  • The publisher cannot say when the block lists were last reviewed against the standard
You may want to confirm that the live blocks and the written standard agree line for line. One approach is to place the current standard next to the active blocks in the Index Exchange console and reconcile the two, adding blocks for commitments that lack one and removing blocks that no longer reflect a standard.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, direct-sold sponsorships and category exclusivity deals are common, and each one usually carries a commitment to keep a competitor's ads off the relevant sections. You may want to reconcile every active sponsorship and exclusivity term against the live blocks, so a commitment the sales team made is actually enforced against the open auction rather than living only in the contract.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the publisher often sells products that compete with the advertisers bidding on its inventory, so a competitor's ad on a product page can undercut the publisher's own sale. You may want to keep a current block list of direct competitors by advertiser domain and category, and reconcile it whenever the product catalog or a retail partnership changes.

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's written brand-safety standard, then list the active blocks in the Index Exchange console and reconcile the two against each other.

ix_console.label: Index Exchange console
steps: Open 'Blocks & Campaigns', then 'Blocking Controls', and list every active block by type, including advertiser brand, advertiser domain (adomain), industry or product category, creative type, and creative ID.
Place the publisher's current written brand-safety standard alongside the active blocks, including any direct-sold exclusivity or category-sponsorship commitments.
For each line in the standard, confirm a matching block exists, and record any commitment that has no block enforcing it.
For each active block, confirm it still maps to a current line in the standard, and record any block that references a lapsed advertiser, category, or commitment.
Ask the publisher when the blocks were last reviewed against the standard, and record the date.

served_creative_review.label: Served-creative review
steps: Pull a sample of creatives that served on the inventory over a recent period from the Index Exchange reporting or ad-quality view.
Check the sampled advertisers, domains, and categories against the written standard to spot any ad that ran but should have been blocked.
Record any served creative that conflicts with the standard, along with the advertiser, domain, or category it falls under.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each line of the publisher's written brand-safety standard alongside the active blocks in the Index Exchange console, and notes whether the two reconcile. A setup where every standard line has a matching block, every active block maps to a current standard line, and the blocks were reviewed recently passes. A setup where commitments lack a matching block, blocks reference lapsed terms, or served creatives conflict with the standard is a fail recorded with the specific gaps and the advertisers or categories affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the written standard, the active blocks, and any served creative that conflicts with the standard above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the creative blocks on {{site_name}}'s Index Exchange (IX) inventory do not reconcile with a current written brand-safety standard, so advertisers, categories, or creative types the publisher meant to exclude can run on its inventory. The platform's default blocks screen out prohibited content, but the blocks that carry the publisher's own standard, including direct-sold exclusivity and category commitments, are the publisher's to maintain, and gaps there stay invisible until an off-brand ad is reported or read from the served-creative data. {{voice_recommend}} placing the current standard next to the active blocks in the Index Exchange console and reconciling the two, adding a block for every commitment that lacks one and removing blocks that no longer reflect the standard. After the reconciliation, set a recurring review so the blocks track the standard as it changes.

audit_checks.title: Blocks are over-broad and suppress more demand than the standard requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block scoped wider than the standard calls for removes demand that the publisher would have accepted, which lowers competition in the auction and the price each impression clears at. Blocking a whole industry category to keep out one advertiser, or applying a block site-wide when the standard only covers one section, suppresses revenue without adding brand-safety protection the publisher needs.

educate.base:
A block protects the inventory by removing demand, and removing demand has a cost. Every advertiser, domain, or category a publisher blocks is one fewer bidder competing for the impression, and fewer bidders means lower competition and a lower clearing price. That cost is worth paying when the block enforces a real standard. It is waste when the block reaches wider than the standard requires. In that case the publisher pays the revenue cost without getting brand-safety protection it actually needs.

Over-broad blocking usually comes from picking too coarse a control. Index Exchange (IX) lets a publisher block at several levels of precision, from a single creative ID or advertiser domain up to a whole industry or product category. When the goal is to keep out one advertiser, blocking that advertiser's brand or domain does the job, while blocking the entire category it belongs to also removes every other advertiser in that category, most of whom the publisher would have accepted. The same pattern shows up with scope. A standard that covers one premium section, applied as a site-wide block, suppresses demand on every other section for no reason the standard supports.

The fix is to match the block to the narrowest control that still enforces the standard. Block the advertiser by brand or domain rather than blocking its category. Scope a block to the ad units the standard covers rather than the whole property. Where the concern is a product category, the IAB Ad Product Taxonomy gives a standardized way to name the category precisely, so the block names what the standard actually excludes rather than a broad bucket that catches more than intended.

A few signs point to blocks that are wider than the standard requires:
  • A whole industry or product category is blocked when the standard only names a specific advertiser or two within it
  • A block applies site-wide when the underlying standard covers only one section or placement
  • Reporting shows a category or advertiser with strong demand elsewhere contributing little or nothing on this inventory because of a block
  • The publisher cannot tie a given block back to a specific line in the written standard
You may want to confirm that each block is scoped to the narrowest control that still enforces the standard. One approach is to review each broad block against the standard line it serves, then replace it with a tighter advertiser-level or section-level block wherever the broad version is catching demand the standard does not exclude.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a category sponsorship usually requires blocking competitors in one section, not across the whole property. You may want to scope each competitor block to the sponsored section's ad units, so the rest of the site keeps that category's demand and the clearing price it brings rather than losing it to a site-wide block.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, blocking a broad product category to keep out a few competing retailers also removes non-competing advertisers in that category who would have bid. You may want to block the specific competing advertiser domains instead, so the inventory keeps the wider category demand while still keeping direct competitors off the pages that matter.

investigate.intro: Review each broad block in the console against the standard line it is meant to enforce, and use reporting to see how much demand each block removes.

ix_console.label: Index Exchange console
steps: Open 'Blocks & Campaigns', then 'Blocking Controls', and list the active blocks, noting the level of each, such as creative ID, advertiser domain, advertiser brand, or industry or product category.

For each category-level or site-wide block, find the line in the written standard it is meant to enforce, and record whether a narrower advertiser-level or section-level block would enforce the same line.
Record any block that cannot be tied to a specific standard line because a block with no standard behind it removes demand for no stated reason.

ix_reporting.label: Index Exchange reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by advertiser category and by advertiser, with bid requests, win rate, and revenue, across the inventory and over a recent period.
For each broad block, look at how the blocked category or advertiser performs on comparable unblocked inventory to estimate the demand and revenue the block removes.
Note any broad block that removes meaningful demand the standard does not actually require excluding, which points to a block that could be scoped tighter.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each broad block alongside the standard line it enforces and the demand it removes, then judges whether a narrower control would enforce the same line. A setup where each block is scoped to the narrowest control that still meets the standard passes. A setup where category-level or site-wide blocks remove demand the standard does not require excluding, or where a block has no standard behind it, is a fail recorded with the blocks and the demand they suppress.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each broad block, the standard line it serves, and the demand it removes above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some creative blocks on {{site_name}}'s Index Exchange (IX) inventory are scoped wider than the brand-safety standard requires, so they remove demand the publisher would have accepted and lower the price each impression clears at. Blocking a whole category to keep out one advertiser, or applying a block site-wide when the standard covers one section, suppresses revenue without adding protection the standard calls for. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each broad block against the standard line it serves, then replacing it with a tighter advertiser-level or section-level block wherever the broad version catches demand the standard does not exclude. After the change, recheck the affected category and advertiser demand to confirm the tighter block keeps the standard while restoring the competition the broad block had removed.
